title: Anti-Gay Laws and Corruption in Africa
summary: Africa's better days lie with a more tolerant future, not a past world that seeks to have a stranglehold on society by globalizing hate and entrenching it in national legislation.
body: Note This commentary originally appeared on the author's FaceBook page. This commentary was reposted on allAfrica.com on January 21, 2014, as " Anti-Gay Laws Will Heighten Corruption in Africa ." The latest from Belfer See more The spread and strengthening of anti-gay laws in Africa (now covering 39 out of 54 countries on the continent) is to threat everyone's human rights irrespective of their sexual orientation. Because of the public stigma attached to being gay, the laws can be used to extract false confessions and bribes. This happened to me in Kenya in the early 1980s. My organization, Environment Liaison Centre International—then chaired by the late Nobel laureate Wangari Maathai—hosted the founder of a small start-up NGO in Sweden called Ekoteket. He had hosted me on my visit to Sweden. On his last day I offered to buy his dinner and show him around in Nairobi prior to his late flight. On the way to his hotel around 8:30 PM to pick up his luggage to go to the airport we were stopped by two policemen. We were asked for our IDs which we proudly produced. I explained who we were and the purpose of our guest's visit. After inspecting our documents, one of the officers pulled me aside and said if we didn't give them some money they would arrest us and we would be charged for breaking the law on homosexuality. Suddenly it dawned on me that I could not prove that I was not a male prostitute. Where there is stigma a charge is the punishment, no further proof is needed to inflict injury. I was stunned by the claim and the humiliation of having to say this to our guest. I tried again to explain, including the fact our guest had used up all his local currency and all he had left was money for his taxi. I only had money for my taxi home that evening. I ended giving up my taxi fare and a little more from my guest. We had to show the policemen all the money we had. My worst memory of the encounter was being forced by the policemen to shake hands saying we gave them the money in good faith. When I recounted to Wangari Maathai this experience she related it to her own ordeals and the abuse of laws that discriminate against women. For her this was yet another example of the dangers of discriminatory practices and why people needed to oppose them even if they didn't think they affected them directly. The spread of anti-gay laws is a threat to every African irrespective of his or her sexual orientation. They broaden the foundation for corruption and encourage the trampling of liberties. Africa's better days lie with a more tolerant future, not a past world that seeks to have a stranglehold on society by globalizing hate and entrenching it in national legislation. My conscience and personal experience advise against silence. This is because in the hands of determined corrupt officials I cannot prove that I am not gay. This in itself is a test no one should be subjected to in a society that cherishes human liberty. Statements and views expressed in this commentary are solely those of the author and do not imply endorsement by Harvard University, the Harvard Kennedy School, or the Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs. Recommended citation Juma, Calestous. "Anti-Gay Laws and Corruption in Africa." Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs, Harvard Kennedy School, January 20, 2014. _list.source: Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs, Harvard Kennedy School

result.main_content_en: The article argues that the proliferation of anti-gay laws across Africa, present in 39 out of 54 countries, poses a broad threat to human rights and fuels corruption. The author shares a personal anecdote from 1980s Kenya where police used the stigma and illegality of homosexuality to extort a bribe, threatening false charges. This illustrates how such discriminatory laws provide a tool for corrupt officials to target anyone, as innocence is difficult to prove against a stigmatized accusation. The late Wangari Maathai connected this to the abuse of laws discriminating against women. The author concludes that these laws undermine liberties for all Africans, regardless of sexual orientation, and that a tolerant future, not one entrenched in legislated hate, is essential for Africa's progress.
result.main_content_zh: 本文论述了非洲反同性恋法律的扩散（在54个国家中的39个存在）对普遍人权构成广泛威胁并助长腐败。作者分享了20世纪80年代在肯尼亚的个人经历：警察利用同性恋的污名化和非法性，以捏造指控相威胁进行敲诈勒索。这一事例说明，此类歧视性法律为腐败官员提供了针对任何人的工具，因为在污名化指控面前自证清白极为困难。已故的旺加里·马塔伊将此与歧视妇女的法律滥用相联系。作者总结认为，这些法律损害了所有非洲人的自由，无论其性取向如何。非洲的美好未来在于一个更加包容的社会，而非一个通过将仇恨全球化并固化为国家立法来扼杀社会的过去世界。
